author: Jordi Serra
body: Barcelona's 0-2 first-leg loss to Atlético was not a night without the ball. Barca finished with 58.3 percent possession, 18 shot attempts, seven on target, and seven corners. What they lost was the match's most expensive stretch: Pau Cubarsí went off in the 44th minute, Julián Álvarez scored in the 45th, and Alexander Sørloth added the second on 70. The tie turned on who owned the penalty areas once the game stopped being comfortable.

Hansi Flick's XI told the story of the original plan. Joan García started behind João Cancelo, Gerard Martín, Cubarsí, and Jules Koundé; Pedri and Eric García formed the first midfield line; Marcus Rashford, Dani Olmo, and Lamine Yamal worked behind Robert Lewandowski. For 40 minutes Barcelona could still pin Atlético back. After the sending-off, the distances changed: the back line had one fewer clean exit, Pedri had too much ground to solve, and the next attacks started farther from the spaces where Olmo and Yamal can actually hurt a defense.

The halftime double switch mattered because it admitted that the first idea had already broken. Pedri and Lewandowski went off for Gavi and Fermín López, which gave Barcelona more running but less control over where the game was being played. That is the real contrast with the 2-1 league win at Atlético on Apr 4. In that match Rashford scored at 42, Lewandowski won it at 87, and Nicolás González's dismissal at 45+7 tilted the chaos toward Barcelona. The European first leg reversed the same leverage.

That is why Espanyol on Apr 11 cannot be treated as administrative work before Madrid. Barcelona need a derby that restores the relationships around the ball: Yamal receiving in advantage, Olmo closer to the box, Rashford attacking the far channel, and Pedri dictating the first clean pass if he starts. The attack still has numbers behind it — Yamal has 14 league goals and nine assists in 26 league appearances, Dani Olmo has seven goals and seven assists, Rashford has five goals and six assists, Lewandowski has 12 league goals, and Pedri has seven assists — but the return leg on Apr 14 only becomes manageable if Barca spend the weekend proving they can turn possession back into pressure again.
conviction: Barcelona's problem in the first leg was not abstract mentality; it was losing the decisive spaces once Cubarsí went off.
dek: The 0-2 first-leg loss was defined by Pau Cubarsí's red card, the two Atlético finishes that followed, and a Barcelona side that produced volume without turning it into authority.
excerpt: Barcelona had the ball often enough in the first leg. Atlético owned the decisive minutes and the decisive spaces.
headline: Barcelona still had the ball. Atlético had the decisive minutes.
heroCaption: Volume was there; control of the decisive moments was not.

metaDescription: A current-week analysis of Barcelona's 0-2 first-leg loss to Atlético, from Cubarsí's red card to the Espanyol reset and return leg in Madrid.
pullQuote: Barcelona kept the ball often enough. The expensive moments did not stay blaugrana.
ritual: One league win in Madrid, one red card at home, one derby now asked to reset the tone.
seoTitle: Barcelona still had the ball. Atlético had the decisive minutes. | totalBarca

timeline.note: Rashford and Lewandowski scored in a 2-1 league win at Atlético after Nicolás González was sent off.
Cubarsí saw red in the 44th minute before Álvarez and Sørloth scored in the first leg.
Espanyol is the immediate reset before the Apr 14 return leg in Madrid.
